data.title: Trump signs bill to end US shutdown, China dominates patent race: SCMP daily highlights
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China stories of the day. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Trump signs funding bill to end longest US government shutdown
US President Donald Trump signed a bill to end the record-breaking US government shutdown after the House of Representatives approved a bipartisan funding deal on Wednesday.
2. China dominates global patent race with record filings
China submitted the highest number of patent applications in 2024, leading global intellectual property activity, a new report shows.
3. Meet Ma Weiming, the Chinese naval genius behind the Fujian’s catapult

Only a select few People’s Liberation Army officers attended last week’s commissioning of the Fujian, but among them was Rear Admiral Ma Weiming – the man whose technological breakthrough put the aircraft carrier among the most advanced of its kind.
4. Has Takaichi planted a Taiwan ‘time bomb’ in Tokyo’s ties with Beijing?
Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s suggestion that Japan could deploy its military in response to a Taiwan contingency has escalated diplomatic tensions and risks further damaging bilateral ties, analysts have warned.
5. Images suggest China is building its first nuclear-powered carrier

A week after China commissioned its latest aircraft carrier, the Fujian, newly leaked images, including satellite imagery and other photos, are among the first signs that China may be building a carrier to be propelled by nuclear reactors.
6. ‘Not enthusiastic’: why China finds Trump’s ‘G2’ talk too costly in geopolitics
While the term treats China and the US as equals, Beijing is probably “not enthusiastic” about its “hegemonic” undertones, analysts say.
7. US outlines agreement with China to curb fentanyl chemicals as tariffs ease

US President Donald Trump’s administration has disclosed further details about its agreement with China on the control of fentanyl precursors, key ingredients in the production of the drug that kills tens of thousands of Americans each year.

data.title: China-US trade war seen growing more volatile despite truce, as both court third countries
data.description:
China-US relations are likely to become more volatile in the coming years, with both sides competing to win over third countries – a dynamic already reshaping global supply chains, according to analysts.
“We’re going to see cycles of ups and downs. They’re going to test each other,” said Scott Kennedy, senior adviser at the US think tank Centre for Strategic and International Studies, at a forum in Beijing on Thursday. “What we’ve seen over time is the amplitude of those cycles that has got higher and higher since 2018.”
During a session on the multipolar world at the 2025 Caixin Summit, Kennedy noted that both nations remain deeply interdependent despite possessing tools capable of inflicting economic pain on each other.
Following the “small deal” reached after the two presidents met last month, a temporary truce – lasting a year or more – could create space for further negotiations, he added.
But he also warned that domestic political pressures could push US President Donald Trump towards a more hawkish stance, leveraging new tools and tougher policies.
As the world’s two largest economies remain locked in tensions and uncertainty, the rest of the world has not remained immune to the fallout.




Joerg Wuttke, a partner at consulting firm DGA Albright Stonebridge Group in Washington and the former president of the European Union Chamber of Commerce in China, warned that Europe is caught between the two superpowers, calling the situation “really worrisome”.
He cited Beijing’s recent export controls on rare earths – measures not directed at European companies but still causing collateral damage.
“So in a way, it becomes clearer that we produce in the US for the US. We produce in China, for China. We produce in Europe, basically for Europe, but also for the rest of the world,” Wuttke said.
In response to the trade war, he noted that companies are redesigning their supply chains, prompting a reassessment of investments in China and the US as well as a search for alternatives.
Beyond supply chains, Kennedy said other countries are hesitant to commit themselves fully to one side, calling Washington’s flurry of agreements with other nations a “boil-the-frog” strategy to gradually isolate China.
This is unlikely to succeed, he said, as these nations have their own business interests while massive lay-offs in the US government have left it with limited capacity to implement all the deals.
Kennedy stressed that despite both sides working hard to win allies, the “pick-a-side” game would not work. “China and the US are going to have to come up with more carrots and fewer sticks. If they want to win, at the end of the day, it’s going to be who’s offering better value to the rest of the world.”

data.title: Ng waiting for next generation of Hong Kong track stars to ease the burden of pursuit
data.description:
Ng Pak-hang has said the emergence of a promising crop of youngsters would end the current situation of Hong Kong’s leading cyclists being asked to spread themselves too thin.
The 26-year-old, who finished fourth in last week’s National Games time trial, was part of the team pursuit quartet eliminated in qualifying on a fruitless opening day for the city’s athletes.
Ng said he and teammates Vincent Lau Wan-yau, Chu Tsun-wai and Mow Ching-yin had not prioritised training for the pursuit, owing to their road commitments and the fact that Chu is a Madison medal hope.
“With more riders coming through, we’ll be able to have track and road specialists and fully focus on our own events,” Ng said at the Hong Kong Velodrome.
“With not so many riders, we’ve had to put in the effort on the road and track, which is quite difficult.”

The 16-year-old Lee Wan-chun won road race gold at the recent Asian Youth Games, while Daniel Yip Hon-man claimed 2025 Asian Road Cycling Championships junior road race gold.
Herve Dagorne, the head coach, has already said the 19-year-old pair of Cheung Shi-ho and Wu Chun-yin will join the senior squad next year, while Ng and Mow both tipped the prodigious Ma Yik-fei, 18, as the next big talent off the block.
“In future, I think we could have six or seven riders focusing on the team pursuit,” Ng said. “The coach tells us we have to improve by one second every year, then in four years we’ll reach our goal.”
Hong Kong finished ninth out of 13 in qualifying, just 0.05 seconds behind Jilin in the last quarter-final spot.
Ng said the aim had been to clock close to four minutes, which “has been our target time for years”, but they completed the 4,000 metres in four minutes and 4.387 seconds.
Lau said he had quickly refocused after a double disappointment last week. A fall in the time trial ruined his medal chances, then he faded in the road race after twice trying to launch attacks in the closing 40km.
“I don’t let my emotions take control of me, I always quickly debrief and regroup,” Lau said. “It’s always special to compete in Hong Kong, it wasn’t the most crowded [in the one-third full stands] but when we were on the track the noise was huge.”
While Lau was looking forward to a holiday in Korea and “eating foods I usually avoid, like carbs and sweets, because I worry about getting fat”, Chu turned his sights to Sunday’s Madison, where he will partner Tso Kai-kwong.
The 24-year-old, who was 15th in the road race, said: “I’ll recover in time, I’ll have massages and I hope to compete for a medal.”

The women’s team pursuit foursome finished qualifying down in 12th after stopping the clock at 4:34.764.
Boey Leung Bo-yee called the performance satisfactory, nonetheless, given star turn Ceci Lee Sze-wing was rested for her Madison and omnium medal bids. Chloe Leung Wing-yee, Yang Qianyu and Kwan Tsz-kwan completed the team.
“Our time was better than expected, but we’re trying to improve,” said Leung, who revealed the team were “still waiting” for the champagne promised by head coach Herve Dagorne after they helped pilot Lee to road race gold on Sunday.
Junior team member Kwan said she was “nervous before the race, but I had my teammates, who are big sisters, helping me and talking about techniques and details in the race.”
The Hong Kong men and women sprint teams both bowed out in qualifying.

data.title: QS university rankings ridiculed in China as latest listing bombs online
data.description:
A university ranking that once had the reputation in China as one of the world’s most authoritative measures of higher education has become the subject of ridicule on social media.
The QS World University Rankings, produced by the London-based education consultancy Quacquarelli Symonds since 2004, has long been regarded as a “gold standard” for comparing universities worldwide.
Even today, some Chinese companies and local governments use it as a main criterion for hiring and granting residency status.
But the newly released 2026 QS Asia University Rankings took many by surprise. Both its results and methodology have raised eyebrows on Chinese social media, with many users calling the list “nonsense” or “unintentionally funny”.
In prime position was the University of Hong Kong, followed by Peking University at No 2 and NTU Singapore in third place.
Also in the top 10 were the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology at No 6, City University of Hong Kong and the Chinese University of Hong Kong at equal seventh, and the Hong Kong Polytechnic University in 10th place.
The ranking also placed Shanghai’s Fudan University at No 5, well ahead of Tsinghua’s ninth position.

This puzzled commenters because Tsinghua University is widely regarded as one of China’s leading universities and consistently ranks higher than Fudan and universities in Hong Kong across major global systems.
Even more jaw-dropping results came from outside China. Japan’s University of Tokyo – the region’s academic powerhouse and home to over a dozen Nobel laureates – ranked only 26th.
Meanwhile, three lesser-known universities in Malaysia – Universiti Malaya, Universiti Putra Malaysia and Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia – all came in ahead, placing 15th, 22nd and 24th respectively.
The apparent inconsistency between the regional and global lists also baffled many social media users. In the global QS rankings, the University of Tokyo is at No 36 while the three Malaysian universities all sit well below it.
“So how does Universiti Malaya end up ahead of Tokyo in the Asia-only ranking?” one social media commenter wrote. “What kind of logic is that?”
“Blaming this ranking on an intern would be an insult to interns,” another joked. “It looks more like the work of an AI model hallucinating its way through the data.”
Quacquarelli Symonds did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
According to the company’s website, its global rankings place a strong emphasis on research performance, with citations per faculty making up 20 per cent of the total score.


However, internationalisation accounts for 25 per cent in the Asia-specific rankings, covering everything from the share of international faculty to the proportion of exchange students.
The result is a system that favours globally connected universities, especially in English-speaking or Commonwealth countries, while putting large research powerhouses like Tsinghua or the University of Tokyo at a disadvantage.
Education experts have raised questions over the years about the QS ranking’s methodology, including its heavy reliance on reputational surveys, which together account for half of both its global and regional rankings.
The issue was highlighted in a 2013 Inside Higher Ed report, which continues to be widely cited.
In the online discussion in China, a social media user questioned whether a university ranking system that relied 50 per cent on subjective surveys and another 20 per cent on internationalisation “could really be objective”.
The same commenter wrote that rankings could swing dramatically whenever the weightings changed and that universities eager to climb the list could simply tailor their strategies to the metrics.
The university rankings were “a double-edged sword”, the social media user noted. “They can help people gauge academic strength but commercial rankings are ultimately a business – and inevitably inflated.”
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: 7 doctors contract hand, foot and mouth disease at Hong Kong public hospital
data.description:
Seven doctors at a Hong Kong public hospital have contracted hand, foot and mouth disease (HFMD), the Hospital Authority said on Thursday.
The outbreak of the viral disease occurred at Princess Margaret Hospital in Kwai Chung. Seven doctors from the department of medicine and geriatrics and of surgery, had developed symptoms, including fever, sore throat and rash, since November 2.
“They were diagnosed with hand, foot and mouth disease. One of them has recovered and resumed duty, while the rest remain on leave in stable condition,” the authority said.
“Staff members have complied with infection control guidelines while on duty, including wearing surgical masks and observing good hand hygiene. No patient has been affected.”
The authority said its preliminary investigation found the doctors had been in contact at various gatherings. Follow-up investigations to identify the source of the infection were under way.
HFMD is a common disease in children, caused by enteroviruses such as coxsackieviruses and enterovirus 71 (EV71).
The disease is clinically characterised by the presence of rashes or lesions typically found on the palms and soles, and occasionally on other areas such as the buttocks and thighs, with development of mouth ulcers.
In some cases, patients may only present with painful ulcers at the back of the mouth, a condition known as herpangina, without the more common hand or foot rashes.
Transmission primarily occurs through direct contact with an infected person’s saliva, nose and throat discharge, or the fluid from blisters.
Dr Joseph Tsang Kay-yan, co-chairman of the Hong Kong Medical Association’s advisory committee on communicable diseases, said that while HFMD was relatively less common in adults than in children, it was still possible for them to contract the virus if they were exposed.
“For seven doctors to contract the virus at the same hospital, potential sources of infection may include social gatherings where food might have been shared, or communal spaces like tea rooms or changing rooms,” Tsang said.
He noted an increase in cases in recent years caused by specific enterovirus types, such as EV71 and coxsackievirus A6, which might contribute to a rise in adult infections due to the virus’ potential to mutate.

Tsang said the public need not be unduly worried about adult cases, which were occasionally diagnosed. Nonetheless, he stressed the importance of observing strict personal hygiene practices.
Dr Wilson Lam Wai-shun, president of the Hong Kong Society for Infectious Diseases, said the Hospital Authority needed to investigate the department wards where the infected doctors worked.
“Anyone who has contact with children, whether they are parents, teachers, or doctors, has the potential to become infected. It is rare for adults to contract the virus, though they still face some risk. The most important thing is to see whether the situation affects the patients,” Lam said.

data.title: Netflix drama Had I Not Seen the Sun review: ridiculous serial killer romance from Taiwan
data.description:
2.5/5 stars
Lead cast: Tseng Jing-hua, Moon Lee
Netflix’s latest Taiwanese drama series, Had I Not Seen the Sun, follows a convicted serial killer (played by Tseng Jing-hua) who agrees to a documentary interview after years of speculation surrounding his motives.
Chen Chi-chiang plays Pin-yu, the young crew member who manages to persuade the killer Li Jen-yao to open up, while Moon Lee and Lyan Cheng co-star as pivotal characters from his tumultuous youth.


It has been nine years since teenager Jen-yao embarked on a frenzied killing spree, butchering several of his classmates in a series of grisly attacks that prompted the media to dub him “The Rainstorm Killer”. He turned himself in and was convicted soon afterwards, but Jen-yao never revealed his motive.
Now it is 2023, and documentarians Ta Wei (Umin Boya) and Chien Yi (Sonia Yuan) have been trying in vain to secure an interview with the murderer, that is, until their timid assistant Pin-yu asks. Surprisingly, her request is accepted immediately, and from her very first meeting with Jen-yao, sparks fly through the safety glass.
Not everything is going well for Pin-yu. She recently moved into a new flat, discovering later that the previous tenant was a young woman who “died up the mountain”. She soon begins to experience nightmares, in which she is pursued by a hooded killer, as well as lucid visions of this same girl (Lee).
Pin-yu confides in her best friend, Mu Shen (Cheng), who provides emotional support but neglects to reveal that she actually knew Jen-yao at school.

Had I Not Seen the Sun soon begins to show its hand. While it may initially present as a serial killer mystery thriller, the show is far more invested in being a teenage drama, as four students from wildly different backgrounds are thrown together and torn apart repeatedly under increasingly volatile circumstances.
The series will air on Netflix in two batches, with the first 10 episodes premiering on November 13, followed by the remainder of the series on December 11.
The bulk of this first instalment takes place in 2007, while Jen-yao, Hsiao-tung (Lee), and Mu Shen (who in 2007 was known as Yun-chen), are classmates at a fiercely strict military-run high school in Taichung.
Jen-yao’s home life is perpetually disruptive. His father (Chris Lung) is an abusive drunk who beats his wife (Ann Lee) and is deep in debt to local gangster Big K (Yao Chun-yao). This causes Jen-yao to clash violently with his parents, as well as Big K.

He is also bullied at school by Ouyang Ti (Shih Chih-tian), son of a shady politician who also happens to be Big K’s boss.
Ouyang has his eye on Hsiao-tung, a beautiful, high-achieving student who is training to become a ballerina. One day, she takes pity on Jen-yao and shares her lunch with him, sparking a friendship that soon becomes a romance.
This not only angers Ouyang but also upsets Yun-chen, who is secretly attracted to Hsiao-tung. She tries to warn Hsiao-tung away from Jen-yao, suggesting that he is bad news, but only succeeds in encouraging Hsiao-tung further.
Over the course of 10 episodes, show creators Chien Chi-feng, Chiang Chi-cheng and Lin Hsin-huei construct a narrative of adolescent hardship and emotional turmoil that might go some way towards justifying Jen-yao’s homicidal actions.

Blackmail, extortion, drug abuse, domestic violence, sexual assault and good old-fashioned beatings are all introduced to stoke the fires of motivation, driving a number of characters to the brink in an escalating maelstrom of worst-case scenarios.
Added to all of this is the increasingly bizarre series of events being experienced by Pin-yu in the present, which are veering into full-blown supernatural territory – a baffling departure from even the most preposterous events unfolding during the show’s lengthy flashbacks.
This “everything but the kitchen sink” approach to storytelling is par for the course with Taiwanese thrillers such as this one, as seemingly superfluous supporting characters hijack the narrative with alarming regularity, while almost everybody harbours vital secrets that are only revealed at the last possible moment.
Tseng makes for a suitably tortured and dreamy protagonist, whether staring forlornly into the evening rain or taking yet another crippling beating from his numerous enemies.

Lee makes for a convincingly virtuous romantic interest, while Chen feels poised to take Pin-yu spiralling over the edge in the episodes still to come, as her character must contend with the advances of both an incarcerated psychopath and a spectral presence from beyond the grave.
Almost nothing that unspools in Had I Not Seen the Sun makes much logical sense, but the show delivers just enough sporadic flourishes of violence, drama, romance and genuine intrigue between the long stretches of teen-drama tedium to keep committed viewers on the hook for now.
Had I Not Seen the Sun is streaming on Netflix.

data.title: Tencent expands into AI ‘world models’ as tech giants chase spatial intelligence
data.description:
Chinese tech giant Tencent Holdings is laying the groundwork for what many see as the next frontier of artificial intelligence by investing in “world models” that simulate the physical environment, according to its head of 3D generation and world modelling.
China’s most valuable tech conglomerate joins a fast-growing cohort that includes Google DeepMind and Elon Musk’s xAI in exploring “spatial intelligence”, which some experts have described as “AI’s next great frontier”.
On Wednesday, AI pioneer Li Fei-Fei’s new spatial intelligence start-up, World Labs, launched its first commercial world-model product, Marble.
World models went beyond the “language-centric” intelligence of large language models (LLMs) such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT and DeepSeek’s R1 to offer “true visual and spatial intelligence”, said Guo Chunchao, a principal research scientist of Tencent who is the head of 3D Generation and World Modelling at its model development team, Hunyuan.
By enabling models to understand 3D geometry and predict physical interactions, developers can train AI agents and robots capable of navigating real-world settings.
“World models allow agents to learn complex tasks efficiently and safely within their own simulations, avoiding risky real-world trial and error,” said Guo, who is scheduled to give the keynote speech at the SIGGRAPH Asia conference in Hong Kong in December..
“In essence, we’re building the foundational understanding of the physical world that intelligent systems need to truly generalise.”
Tencent has open-sourced several of its world models in recent weeks – including HunyuanWorld 1.0, HunyuanWorld-Voyager and FlashWorld – allowing developers to generate explorable, interactive 3D worlds from a single image or text prompt.
Some of these models have shot to the top of the trending leaderboard on the open-source developer platform Hugging Face, reflecting strong uptake in the global AI community.

“By building a collaborative ecosystem, we believe the technology will advance far more rapidly,” said Guo, adding that “quantised” versions allowed developers to run the models on consumer-grade hardware.
Shenzhen-based Tencent had integrated world models into its robotics training platform, Tairos, as well as its core gaming business to improve the efficiency of virtual-world generation, he said.
In a report last month, the operator of the world’s largest gaming business said its in-house AI tools now matched the quality of its human video game artists, while broader AI integration had delivered more than a 20 per cent boost in companywide research and development efficiency.
Another AI heavyweight, Yann LeCun, is also reportedly resigning as Meta Platforms’ chief AI scientist to launch a world-model start-up.
Leading the field is Google DeepMind, whose world model Genie 3 made Time magazine’s list of best inventions in 2025. The model was released in August to a small group of academics and creators amid concerns over safety and misuse.
These risks remain one of the major barriers to using world models for large-scale robotics training, according to Guo.
Without reliable and consistent physics simulation, he added, robots trained in flawed virtual simulations could make costly mistakes in the real world.
“The models must learn that objects can’t pass through walls or defy gravity over extended periods,” said Guo.

data.title: Can China and India turn tactical calm into strategic moderation?
data.description:
In recent months, India-China relations have returned to normality after a long decade of confrontation. With the resumption of direct flights after five years, recent corps commander-level talks, the reopening of the Kailash Mansarovar pilgrimage route, the relaxation of barriers on hi-tech supplies and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and President Xi Jinping exchanging pleasantries at the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO) summit, a quiet detente is under way.
This cooperation could be a product of systemic necessity driven by US pressure and tariff realignments. Unless more concrete steps are taken, it might ultimately be a pause in strategic competition.
The material logic for a pause is strong on both sides as India needs China to fulfil its industrial ambitions. Chinese electronics, electric vehicle batteries, rare earth magnets and pharmaceutical materials are essential for boosting India’s manufacturing sector. Reports suggest that Indian conglomerates such as Adani and JSW are engaging with Chinese firms over technology partnerships.
China is India’s second-largest trading partner after the United States. As Indian consumers buy many Chinese goods, bilateral trade reached about US$127.7 billion last year, out of which more than US$113 billion was Chinese exports to India.
India’s vast consumer market is an opportunity for Chinese firms facing a slowing domestic economy. Be it smartphones, cars or digital services, Chinese companies have invested heavily in Indian start-ups and are attached to India’s growing digitalisation. Last year, Indian consumers purchased almost 156 million smartphones, with Chinese brands such as Xiaomi and Vivo capturing the bulk of those sales.
India has emerged as the world’s third-largest car market, with around 4.3 million vehicles sold last year. Chinese EV giant BYD seems to believe this is its next big frontier and has publicly targeted up to a 40 per cent share of India’s EV market. Even if strategic distrust persists, the economic incentives for closer ties are huge, steering the two sides towards being more pragmatic.


The deterioration in India’s ties with the US serves as an important catalyst for this thaw. In addition to Washington imposing higher tariffs on Indian exports than on Chinese goods, the Trump administration has sharply increased fees for H-1B skilled worker visas. The US-India strategic partnership that emerged during the Obama and Biden presidencies is quickly evaporating.
The more transactional direction in India’s relationship with the US weakens its incentive to align with Washington and creates more reason to keep its diplomatic options open when it comes to China. The unravelling of US-India ties creates room for Beijing to see whether economic incentives can help in forming stable ties with New Delhi.
Both sides have started to reduce friction with calculated steps. Delhi has resumed issuing tourist visas to Chinese nationals after five years, while Beijing has relaxed restrictions on urea exports to India. Senior ministers have also started travelling again, with Foreign Minister Wang Yi visiting India after a three-year absence and Indian Foreign Secretary Subrahmanyam Jaishankar making his first trip to Beijing since 2020.
During their meeting at the SCO summit in Tianjin, Modi and Xi discussed trade balancing, people-to-people exchanges, counterterrorism and more. These small but important actions show that Beijing and Delhi are testing the waters with cooperation on low politics to establish a level of diplomatic insulation, a means of maintaining communication even in the face of hard security concerns.


It is crucial to keep in mind that China and India have already approached a solution for their disputed border. The Political Parameters document, which detailed how a final agreement could be framed, was even approved by both parties in 2005. There has not been a lack of ideas, but momentum towards a solution fell each time mistrust surged. Representatives still meet for discussions, but the border is still militarised and unresolved.
After a year-long pause, the two countries restarted talks at the corps commander level in Ladakh last month and are currently moving forward with the second phase of a three-step process for the border issue. The first stage, disengagement, has been mostly achieved; the second, de-escalation, is being addressed. The final stage will be de-induction.
For this detente to endure, it must become more than a tactical pause. Peace will only become permanent if both sides deliberately reduce the space for miscalculation. That means making the Line of Actual Control less vulnerable to accidents. Only when the border ceases to be a signalling tool and is instead governed by verifiable standards, such as patrol codes, hotline obligations and reduced troop levels, can peace be stable.
Mobility should be normalised by building constituencies that directly benefit from stability. It can be achieved through increasing flight frequency and re-establishing journalist accreditations, as well as multi-entry business and researcher visas. This can be supported by universities, think tanks and business chambers through collaborations in non-sensitive fields such as green technology.


Whether India and China decide to institutionalise caution instead of just practising it will determine the fate of this thaw. Both parties could choose to limit competition, but they are not required to give it up. With good border management, economic undertakings and mobility of common people on both sides, real progress can be made.
Managed rivalry is not a romantic notion of harmony. The realisation that both nations stand to benefit more from stability than from conflict is evident. The current calm could signal the start of a more long-lasting balance if Beijing and Delhi can turn this period of tactical tranquillity into a habit of strategic moderation.

data.title: Bangladesh’s Hasina regrets student bloodshed but slams trial as ‘judicial mockery’
data.description:
Bangladesh’s fugitive former prime minister Sheikh Hasina has expressed regret over the “needless” bloodshed as her administration tried to squash student-led protests that resulted in her losing power after 15 years, but poured scorn on a tribunal due to rule if she committed crimes against humanity by ordering the crackdown.
In a defiant interview with This Week in Asia just days before the verdict in her trial in absentia, the 78-year-old accused the interim government that replaced her of a politically motivated attack, as well as preparing to hold elections without the “consent” of millions of Bangladeshis who still support her Awami League party.
About 1,400 people died last year as state forces opened fire on student protesters after demonstrations which had begun as a rally against job quotas morphed into a violent uprising that drove Hasina and her government from office.
That ended her long stranglehold on power and forced her to take refuge in India.

Since then her regime, decried as autocratic and corrupt by many Bangladeshis, has been replaced by an interim government headed by Nobel laureate Muhammad Yunus, which has set out on ambitious reforms vowing to hold fresh elections by February.
Hasina, part of Bangladesh’s complex political history as the daughter of independence hero Sheikh Mujibur Rahman, headed the Awami League which dominated for more than a decade and steered the economy’s growth.
But as the years went by, accusations mounted of crony capitalism and authoritarianism, including extrajudicial disappearances of political rivals.
The students who drove her from Bangladesh say by the end Hasina was out of touch, insouciant to the needs of the people and surrounded by “yes men” who did her bidding in return for positions or contracts.
Speaking to This Week In Asia from India days before her much-awaited November 17 verdict on charges that her student crackdown amounted to crimes against humanity, she said the initially reasonable protests “darkened into mob violence” prompting the state actions.
“We had to act to maintain basic law and order. From there, things spiralled out of control and many people died needlessly,” Hasina said.
“I regret that very much and I would welcome a proper inquiry into what happened.”


In the months-long trial held in her absence, prosecutors in Bangladesh have alleged that Hasina ordered the use of lethal weapons to security forces against the protesters, claims she denies.
“At no point did I order security forces to fire on crowds,” she said. “My political adversaries have alleged this but have shown no persuasive evidence to back the claim, because there is none.”
Hasina has already been sentenced to six months in prison for contempt of court, and separately faces corruption charges. Other high-ranking officials from her former government also face charges, including the former interior minister – who is a fugitive – and the former chief of police, who is in custody and has already pleaded guilty.
After an initial wave of goodwill, Yunus, the interim leader, has struggled to placate Bangladesh’s competing political factions, including Islamists and student leaders who emerged from the revolution that ousted Hasina.
His administration has accused Awami League loyalists inside the civil service – as well as outside the country – of undermining reforms aimed at rooting out endemic corruption, cronyism and rebuilding the independence of the judges and police.
Bangladesh faces the prospect of more unrest as the Awami League called for a nationwide “lockdown” in protest against her trial over last year’s protests.
Schools in Dhaka and major cities across Bangladesh switched to online classes and examinations, while public transport services were severely disrupted on Thursday as the government heightened security across the country.

On Thursday, Yunus said in a televised address that his administration would hold a referendum on a national charter, which was signed last month by political parties, on the day of the next election in February. The date has not yet been announced.
The referendum, Yunus said, would propose a two-tier parliament in line with the “July National Charter” that seeks to reshape the country’s politics and institutions and give constitutional recognition to the 2024 uprising.
Yunus also repeated his pledge to punish Hasina.
Hasina, however, poured scorn on the tribunal due to rule on her case on Monday, describing it as a political hatchet job driven by her rivals. Prosecutors have called for the death penalty if she is found guilty.
“Bangladesh’s so-called International Criminal Tribunal [ICT] is neither international nor even a tribunal. It is a judicial mockery,” she said. “The ICT is controlled by my political opponents in order to deliver a preordained guilty verdict, in an attempt to nullify the Awami League as a political force.”
Despite fleeing the country, she said she was open to facing a fair trial in a “proper, impartial court”.
Hasina, who won a fourth consecutive term in 2024, said the interim government’s ban on all activities of the Awami League under the country’s Anti-Terrorism Act made a mockery of its democratic credentials ahead of next year’s election.
“An election where the Awami League, which is supported by tens of millions, is banned, is neither free, fair, nor inclusive,” she said.
“Bangladesh badly needs a government ruling with the consent of the people. You will not achieve that if millions of people are disenfranchised. Whether in government or in opposition, the Awami League needs to be part of the political conversation in Bangladesh.”

Outside looking in
From India, where she enjoys the support of Narendra Modi’s government, Hasina has launched a campaign to erode faith in the electoral process Yunus has set course on, accusing his government of exceeding its interim authority.
The South Asian nation has also been trying to stabilise the economy while negotiating emergency loans from multilateral agencies, cut public spending and reform its banking system, which it says has been drained of billions of dollars under Hasina’s time in office.
The country’s mainstay garment sector has, however, been under pressure due to higher import tariffs imposed by Washington, even as unemployment has been rampant since the Covid-19 pandemic.
The situation has been compounded by a more strained relationship with India, which has hampered Bangladesh’s logistics for exporting products such as garments.
New Delhi harbours suspicions that Dhaka may invite Chinese investment close to India’s vulnerable northeast region, as well as court Pakistan for military support.
The Yunus administration has denied trying to undermine Indian interests.

Hasina said Bangladesh needed “strong and constructive relationships” with both China and India.
Under her stewardship, Bangladesh’s economy was a success story marked by significant GDP growth and poverty reduction. But it has also been prone to weaknesses such as structural issues and institutional fragilities exacerbated by global events including the Russia-Ukraine war and the pandemic, as well as rampant corruption inside its banks.
The International Monetary Fund has projected that Bangladesh’s economy will grow by 4.9 per cent in the 2025-26 financial year, an increase from 3.8 per cent the previous year.
“These growth numbers are actually dismayingly small, versus Bangladesh’s recent historic trend – and not enough to sustain our country’s heroic efforts in recent years to lift itself out of poverty,” Hasina said.
The disgraced former leader said under the right circumstances she would return to her country, but ruled out the possibility of her party collaborating with the newly formed National Citizen Party, which has become influential due to its support base among students.
“Our role has always been to serve the people of Bangladesh, and we will continue with this mission in any way we can,” she said. “Whatever happens, we will remain peaceful.”
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Michelle Yeoh, Stray Kids, among star line-up for major K-pop award show in Hong Kong
data.description:
Fans attending Asia’s biggest K-pop awards show to be held at Hong Kong’s Kai Tak Stadium in November can expect to see Oscar-winning actress Michelle Yeoh, the global debut of a new song by South Korean boy band Stray Kids and a special collaboration with the Netflix animated series KPop Demon Hunters.
The event organisers on Wednesday announced the highlights of the 2025 MAMA Awards, featuring performances by more than 25 acts, ranging from rookie groups to K-pop veterans.
The award show, returning to Hong Kong for the first time since 2018, will take place at the city’s 50,000-seat Kai Tak Stadium, four times larger than the previous venue at the Asia-World Arena.
With over 15 awards up for grabs, the show will be held across two days, on November 28 and 29.
The sold-out event is expected to draw K-pop fans from across the world to Hong Kong, including mainland Chinese fans discussing how to travel to the venue on social media platform RedNote.
Oscar-winning actress Michelle Yeoh, renowned for her iconic roles in films such as Everything Everywhere All at Once and Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon, will appear at the event as a presenter.
South Korean actor Park Bo-gum, the host for the first evening, will perform a new interpretation of the traditional Korean song Kwaejina Ching Ching with members of boy groups BOYNEXTDOOR, CORTIS, TREASURE and dance crew BUMSUP.
The star-studded line-up on the first day includes Hong Kong boy band Mirror and South Korean groups idle, IVE, ENHYPHEN and NCT Wish, among others.
Industry veterans Super Junior will also be celebrating their 20th anniversary with a special performance.

K-pop star G-DRAGON will headline the second day of the event, which will also feature performances by aespa, CORTIS, RIIZE and Stray Kids. The latter will perform a new song from their upcoming album for the first time.
The second day will also see a special performance between host Kim Hye-soo and Felix from Stray Kids.
A special performance in collaboration with Netflix’s global hit film KPop Demon Hunters is expected to be a highlight, combining live performance with animation.
Shortlisted acts for Best Female Group include aespa, BABYMONSTER, idle, IVE, TWICE and LE SSERAFIM.
The nominations for the Best Female Artist include three members of K-pop sensation Blackpink: Jennie, Jisoo and Rosé.
Fans who are unable to get their hands on tickets to the sold-out show can watch the live stream via Mnet Plus.
